content.text: Guaranteeing that a language model produces output conforming exactly to a required structure — valid JSON, a specific enum value, a regex-matched string — is possible without any post-generation retry loop.
(also called schema-enforced generation or guided generation) achieves this by intervening

the sampling loop: at every token-generation step, tokens that would make the output inconsistent with the target grammar or schema have their log-probabilities set to −∞ before sampling occurs, making them statistically impossible to select.

Standard LLM decoding works by computing a probability distribution over the entire vocabulary at each step and sampling from it. Every token in the vocabulary has some nonzero probability, including tokens that would produce malformed JSON, wrong field types, or values outside an allowed enum. Post-hoc validation catches these failures
generation is complete and retries, but that wastes compute and adds latency. Constrained decoding eliminates invalid tokens
The constraint is expressed as a formal grammar — most commonly JSON Schema, a context-free grammar (CFG) in EBNF notation, or a regular expression. An

(a pushdown automaton for CFGs, a DFA for regexes) tracks the current parse state based on tokens already emitted. At each generation step, the automaton answers the question: which vocabulary tokens are valid continuations of the current partial output? Any token not in that valid set gets its logit set to −∞; the model then samples only from the remaining valid candidates.
The result is a hard guarantee: the model

produce output that violates the schema, regardless of what the model's internal representations would have preferred. This is stronger than any prompt-based instruction or post-hoc validation because it operates at the mathematical level of the generation process itself.

Agentic pipelines that call downstream APIs, populate databases, or invoke tools all depend on structured output. A single malformed JSON object can crash an entire pipeline, corrupt a database record, or cause a tool call to fail silently. The conventional defense — generate, parse, validate, retry — has several painful properties:
Each retry incurs a full forward pass (often 1–10 s), and failure rates in the 5–20 % range are common without schema-enforced prompting.
Under high temperature or with unusual prompt phrasing, failure rates can spike unpredictably.
Downstream code that expects parsed JSON and receives a string beginning with '```json' raises an unhandled exception.
The model may produce JSON that parses but fails schema validation (wrong type, missing required field), requiring deep inspection to detect.

Constrained decoding collapses all four problems: the output is structurally valid on the first attempt, every time. For batch workloads where even a 1 % failure rate compounds to thousands of retries, or for real-time systems where retry latency is unacceptable, this guarantee is operationally essential.
The generated output always satisfies the grammar on the first pass — no parse errors, no missing required fields, no wrong types.
Eliminates the latency and token cost of regeneration loops.
Downstream code can safely assume the contract without defensive try/except wrappers around every parse call.
Elaborate formatting instructions in the system prompt become unnecessary; the constraint enforces structure mechanically.
The model can spend fewer tokens on structural markers (brackets, colons) whose correctness is now guaranteed, and more on semantic content.
JSON Schema, regex, EBNF, Pydantic models, and custom grammars can all be enforced; the abstraction is not limited to JSON.
Constrained decoding is not free. Each trade-off requires understanding before committing to it in production:

The technique requires inference-engine support (vLLM, llama.cpp, Outlines, TGI). Hosted API providers (OpenAI, Anthropic) offer JSON mode or tool-call schemas, which are high-level analogues, but do not expose raw logit-processor hooks.

The automaton must compute the valid-token mask for each step. For complex JSON Schemas with many properties and nested objects, mask computation can add 5–20 % to time-per-token, depending on the implementation and vocabulary size.

When the model's preferred continuation is always masked out (e.g., the model 'wants' to write an explanation but the schema only permits an integer), the forced valid token may be semantically wrong. The structure is guaranteed; the semantics are not.
Very large or deeply recursive schemas can produce automaton states too numerous to precompute efficiently. Some frameworks cap complexity or require schema simplification.

The valid-token mask is computed against the model's specific tokenizer vocabulary. The same grammar can have very different masking characteristics across models (e.g., GPT-4 tokenizer vs. LLaMA tokenizer). A mask built for one model must be rebuilt for another.
Not a substitute for semantic validation.

A constrained output that is structurally correct may still be semantically wrong: the 'age' field may contain 999, the 'email' field a syntactically valid but fake address. Schema enforcement guarantees structure; business logic validation is still required.
. The constrained decoding layer intercepts these logits before the softmax and applies a binary mask

attrs.latex: l'_i = \begin{cases} l_i & \text{if } m_i = 1 \text{ (token valid in current grammar state)} \\ -\infty & \text{if } m_i = 0 \text{ (token would violate grammar)} \end{cases}

content.text: pass through softmax to produce the sampling distribution. Because
, invalid tokens receive zero probability. The sampled token advances the automaton to a new parse state, which determines the next mask.
Different constraint sources compile down to different automaton types:
Each character/byte transition is precomputed. Mask computation is O(1) per state per token.
The stack tracks nesting depth (object/array levels, current field name context). Mask computation requires stack inspection.
EBNF grammar → PDA or Earley parser.
General context-free grammars require either a chart parser or a PDA. llama.cpp's grammar mode uses a custom incremental PDA.

Tokenizer vocabularies operate over token IDs, not characters. A single schema state may allow the character '1' as a valid continuation, but which tokens correspond to '1'? The framework must precompute, for each grammar state, the set of token IDs whose string representation is a valid prefix in that state. This precomputation (called a token mask or trie walk) dominates the setup cost and is typically cached.
tokens and a grammar with S states, the precomputed table has size O(S × |V|) bits — roughly a few MB for typical JSON Schemas.

The diagram below shows the full constrained decoding pipeline. The key insertion point is the logit processor: a stateful object (the grammar automaton) sits between the raw LLM logit output and the sampling step. Each token emission advances the automaton state, which determines the next mask.

attrs.alt: Constrained Decoding Pipeline — shows LLM forward pass feeding logits into a grammar automaton logit processor that masks invalid tokens before sampling; token emission advances automaton state in a feedback loop

content.text: The grammar automaton is the critical component. It must be:
Incremental — able to accept one token at a time and update its state in O(1) or O(log n) time
Consistent with the tokenizer — mask computation must map grammar characters to token IDs, not raw bytes
Stateful across the full sequence — it must remember the parse context (e.g., 'currently inside the "address" object, expecting the "city" field or closing brace')
High-volume batch structured extraction where a 5% retry rate multiplies to thousands of wasted calls
Real-time APIs where retry latency is unacceptable (< 500 ms SLA)
Tool-call schemas with strict enum values — the model must return exactly one of a finite set of strings
Code generation with syntactic guarantees (generate valid Python function signatures, SQL WHERE clauses, etc.)
Safety-critical pipelines where malformed output causes downstream failures that are expensive to detect and recover from
Open-ended generation (stories, summaries) where no structural constraint is needed — adds overhead for no benefit
Schemas too complex for the automaton to compile efficiently (deeply recursive self-referential schemas)
Environments locked to a hosted API with no logit-processor access (use structured output / tool-call API instead)
When semantic correctness matters more than structural correctness — constrained decoding does not prevent semantically wrong values
Outlines is the most mature Python library for constrained decoding. It integrates with local models via

import outlines
import outlines.models as models
from pydantic import BaseModel
from typing import Literal

# Load a local model (transformers backend)
model = models.transformers("mistralai/Mistral-7B-Instruct-v0.2", device="cuda")

# Define the output schema as a Pydantic model
class ExtractedPerson(BaseModel):
    name: str
    age: int
    role: Literal["engineer", "manager", "analyst", "designer"]
    confidence: float  # 0.0 to 1.0

# Create a structured generator — Outlines compiles the Pydantic model
# to a JSON Schema grammar and builds the logit processor
generator = outlines.generate.json(model, ExtractedPerson)

prompt = """Extract the person information from the following text.
Text: 'Sarah Chen is a 34-year-old senior software engineer at the company.'
Output:""\"

# Generate — guaranteed to return a valid ExtractedPerson JSON string
result = generator(prompt)
person = ExtractedPerson.model_validate_json(result)
print(person)
# ExtractedPerson(name='Sarah Chen', age=34, role='engineer', confidence=0.92)
llama.cpp supports GBNF (a variant of EBNF) grammars directly. Pass the grammar string via

from llama_cpp import Llama, LlamaGrammar

# Load a GGUF model
llm = Llama(model_path="mistral-7b-instruct.Q4_K_M.gguf", n_gpu_layers=-1)

# Define a GBNF grammar for a simple key-value JSON object
GRAMMAR = r"""
root   ::= object
object ::= "{" ws (pair ("," ws pair)*)? "}" ws
pair   ::= string ":" ws value
value  ::= string | number | "true" | "false" | "null" | object | array
array  ::= "[" ws (value ("," ws value)*)? "]" ws
string ::= "\"" ([^"\\\x7F\x00-\x1F] | "\\" (["\\/bfnrt] | "u" [0-9a-fA-F] [0-9a-fA-F] [0-9a-fA-F] [0-9a-fA-F]))* "\"" ws
number ::= ("-"? ([0-9] | [1-9] [0-9]*)) ("." [0-9]+)? ([eE] [-+]? [0-9]+)? ws
ws     ::= [ \t\n]*
"""

grammar = LlamaGrammar.from_string(GRAMMAR)

output = llm(
    "Extract JSON: John is 28 years old.",
    grammar=grammar,
    max_tokens=200,
    temperature=0.1,
)
print(output["choices"][0]["text"])
# {"name": "John", "age": 28}
Implementing a custom logit processor with HuggingFace
For full control, implement a custom

import re
import torch
from transformers import LogitsProcessor, AutoTokenizer, AutoModelForCausalLM, pipeline

class RegexLogitsProcessor(LogitsProcessor):
    """Constrains generation to tokens that keep the output matching a prefix of the regex."""
    def __init__(self, pattern: str, tokenizer):
        self.pattern = pattern
        self.tokenizer = tokenizer
        self.generated = ""

    def __call__(self, input_ids: torch.LongTensor, scores: torch.FloatTensor) -> torch.FloatTensor:
        vocab_size = scores.shape[-1]
        mask = torch.full((vocab_size,), float("-inf"), device=scores.device)

        for token_id in range(vocab_size):
            token_str = self.tokenizer.decode([token_id], skip_special_tokens=True)
            candidate = self.generated + token_str
            # Allow if candidate is a valid prefix of the regex
            if re.fullmatch(self.pattern + ".*", candidate, re.DOTALL):
                mask[token_id] = 0.0  # Allow this token

        return scores + mask

model_name = "gpt2"  # illustrative; use an instruction-tuned model in practice
tokenizer = AutoTokenizer.from_pretrained(model_name)
model = AutoModelForCausalLM.from_pretrained(model_name)

processor = RegexLogitsProcessor(r"[A-Z][a-z]+ [A-Z][a-z]+", tokenizer)
inputs = tokenizer("The person's name is: ", return_tensors="pt")
outputs = model.generate(
    **inputs,
    logits_processor=[processor],
    max_new_tokens=10,
)
print(tokenizer.decode(outputs[0], skip_special_tokens=True))
Example 1 — Extract structured event data with Outlines

"""
Runnable example: extract a calendar event from natural language.
Requires: pip install outlines transformers torch
Model: any local instruction-tuned model (e.g., TinyLlama for testing)
"""
from enum import Enum
from typing import Optional
from pydantic import BaseModel
import outlines
import outlines.models as models

class Priority(str, Enum):
    low = "low"
    medium = "medium"
    high = "high"

class CalendarEvent(BaseModel):
    title: str
    date: str          # ISO 8601 date string
    duration_minutes: int
    location: Optional[str]
    priority: Priority

# For testing without a GPU, use a small model
model = models.transformers("TinyLlama/TinyLlama-1.1B-Chat-v1.0", device="cpu")
generator = outlines.generate.json(model, CalendarEvent)

text = "Schedule a high-priority team standup for 2024-03-15 in room 3B, lasting 30 minutes."
result_json = generator(f"Extract calendar event from: '{text}'. Output JSON:")
event = CalendarEvent.model_validate_json(result_json)

print(f"Title: {event.title}")
print(f"Date: {event.date}")
print(f"Duration: {event.duration_minutes} min")
print(f"Priority: {event.priority.value}")
# Guaranteed: all fields present, priority is one of low/medium/high,
# duration_minutes is an integer, no parse errors possible.

"""
Runnable example: generate a SQL WHERE clause constrained to safe syntax.
Uses Outlines regex mode to prevent SQL injection patterns.
"""
import outlines
import outlines.models as models
import re

model = models.transformers("TinyLlama/TinyLlama-1.1B-Chat-v1.0", device="cpu")

# Constrain to a safe WHERE clause pattern:
# column_name OPERATOR 'value' (single-quoted string literal only)
SAFE_WHERE_PATTERN = (
    r"[a-z_][a-z0-9_]* "   # column name
    r"(=|!=|>|<|>=|<=|LIKE) "  # operator
    r"'[^';\\]{1,100}'"     # quoted string value, no semicolons or escapes
)

generator = outlines.generate.regex(model, SAFE_WHERE_PATTERN)

prompt = "Generate a SQL WHERE clause to find users named Alice:"
clause = generator(prompt, max_tokens=50)
print(f"WHERE {clause}")
# Guaranteed output matches the regex — no UNION, no --, no semicolons,
# no unquoted values that could enable injection.

A document processing pipeline at a legal-tech company ingests 50,000 contracts per day and extracts structured metadata (parties, jurisdiction, effective date, contract type, governing law) for downstream database insertion. The original approach used GPT-4 with a system prompt instructing JSON output, followed by parse-and-retry logic. Observed characteristics:
Parse failure rate: 3–8 % depending on document complexity
Average retries per document: 0.06 (most succeed on first try, a few require multiple retries)
Retry cost: ~7 % of total token spend
After migrating to vLLM with Outlines constrained decoding using a Pydantic model matching the database schema:
Retries eliminated — p99 latency dropped to 6 s
Token cost reduced by ~6 % (no retry calls, shorter prompts without elaborate formatting instructions)
Downstream DB insertion errors dropped from ~400/day to 0 structural errors (semantic validation still catches wrong values)

from pydantic import BaseModel
from typing import Literal, Optional
from datetime import date
import outlines
import outlines.models as models

class ContractMetadata(BaseModel):
    party_a: str
    party_b: str
    jurisdiction: Literal[
        "Delaware", "New York", "California", "England and Wales",
        "Singapore", "Hong Kong", "Other"
    ]
    effective_date: str   # YYYY-MM-DD
    contract_type: Literal[
        "NDA", "SaaS", "Employment", "Consulting", "Supply", "Licensing", "Other"
    ]
    governing_law: str
    auto_renewal: bool

# vLLM backend for production throughput
model = models.vllm("mistralai/Mistral-7B-Instruct-v0.2", gpu_memory_utilization=0.85)
extractor = outlines.generate.json(model, ContractMetadata)

def process_contract(contract_text: str) -> ContractMetadata:
    prompt = f"""Extract structured metadata from this contract excerpt.
Contract:
{contract_text[:4000]}

JSON output:"""
    raw = extractor(prompt, max_tokens=300, temperature=0.0)
    return ContractMetadata.model_validate_json(raw)

Applying character-level regex to a field that should contain natural language forces the model into a tortured valid-prefix walk that produces nonsense. If a field should hold arbitrary text, leave it as an unconstrained string in the schema — JSON string encoding already guarantees it will parse.

# BAD: regex-constraining a description field
class BadSchema(BaseModel):
    title: str
    description: str = Field(pattern=r"[A-Za-z ,.]{10,200}")  # Too restrictive

# GOOD: let description be free text; only constrain categorical/numeric fields
class GoodSchema(BaseModel):
    title: str
    description: str  # free text — JSON string encoding is sufficient
    status: Literal["open", "closed", "pending"]
    priority: int  # JSON number type constraint is enough
Mistake 2 — Conflating structural and semantic validity
Constrained decoding guarantees the output is structurally valid JSON matching the schema. It does not guarantee the values are correct. Always apply semantic validation after extraction:

# After constrained generation:
result = generator(prompt)
parsed = MySchema.model_validate_json(result)

# Structural guarantee: parsed always works, no ValidationError possible.
# But STILL validate semantics:
if not (0 <= parsed.confidence_score <= 1.0):
    raise ValueError("Confidence score out of range")
if parsed.effective_date < date.today().isoformat():
    raise ValueError("Effective date is in the past")

When switching models, the logit mask must be recompiled. A mask built for a LLaMA tokenizer is wrong for a Mistral tokenizer even if both models have the same architecture. Always reconstruct the grammar/processor when changing

# WRONG: reusing a processor compiled for model A with model B
processor_a = outlines.generate.json(model_a, MySchema)
result = processor_a(model_b, prompt)  # incorrect — model_b has different tokenizer

# CORRECT: compile the processor for each model separately
processor_a = outlines.generate.json(model_a, MySchema)
processor_b = outlines.generate.json(model_b, MySchema)
Keep schemas as simple as the task allows.
Each additional schema property, enum value, or nested object increases automaton state count. Flatten nested structures where possible.
Precompile and cache the grammar processor.
Grammar compilation (automaton construction + token mask precomputation) takes 100 ms–2 s. Cache the compiled processor and reuse it across requests.
Constrained decoding guarantees structural correctness but not semantic accuracy. Using greedy decoding (temperature=0) maximises the probability of the semantically correct token at each step.
The automaton will force a valid termination (closing brackets, required fields) at max_tokens if reached. Set max_tokens to 1.5× the expected output length to prevent truncation.
Run business-logic checks (date ranges, value bounds, cross-field consistency) on the parsed output even though structure is guaranteed.
Use Optional fields for optional data.
Required fields force the model to produce a value even when none is present in the source text, causing hallucination. Mark fields Optional[str] = None when the information may be absent.
Monitor per-token latency after enabling constraints.
Add instrumentation to compare time-per-token with and without the logit processor to quantify overhead in your serving environment.

The constrained decoding overhead depends on three factors: grammar complexity (number of automaton states), vocabulary size (number of tokens that must be checked per state), and implementation efficiency. Typical observations:
DFA mask lookup is O(1) after precomputation
Large state space; mask recomputation per step
Chart parser per step; highly implementation-dependent

In batched inference, each sequence in the batch may be at a different grammar state, requiring per-sequence mask computation. This can reduce the GPU utilization benefit of batching. vLLM's constrained decoding implementation handles this by computing masks in parallel across batch items using vectorized state lookup, keeping overhead manageable.
Grammar precompilation is the dominant cost, not per-token masking. Outlines performs this once on
and caches the result keyed by (schema_hash, tokenizer_vocab_hash). In a web service, compile schemas at startup or in a background thread, not per request:
Horizontal scaling: constrained decoding is stateless across requests — each request carries its grammar processor; scale by adding inference workers
Grammar simplification: if a schema has 50 required fields, split extraction into two smaller calls (25 fields each) to reduce per-step state count
Precomputed mask tables: for frequently used schemas, serialize and cache the full (state → token-mask) table to disk to avoid recomputation on restart
Async batching: batch multiple extraction requests sharing the same schema to maximize GPU utilization despite per-sequence state divergence

The most feature-complete Python library. Supports JSON Schema, Pydantic models, regex, EBNF, choice (enum), and type-constrained generation. Backends: transformers, vLLM, llama.cpp, OpenAI-compatible APIs (JSON mode). pip install outlines.
Built-in grammar support via GBNF notation and native JSON Schema conversion (--json-schema flag). Available in the C++ binary and Python bindings (llama-cpp-python). No extra dependencies.
vLLM's SamplingParams accepts guided_json, guided_regex, and guided_grammar. Uses xgrammar (formerly lm-format-enforcer) as backend for efficient mask computation.
A standalone logit-processor library that integrates with any HuggingFace generate() call. Accepts JSON Schema and regex. pip install lm-format-enforcer.

A higher-level library that patches OpenAI / Anthropic clients to return validated Pydantic models. Does not perform constrained decoding (uses retry loops internally) but provides a clean interface for the hosted-API tier.
Supports grammar constraints via the --grammar parameter in the /generate endpoint. Backend uses lm-format-enforcer.

A new high-performance grammar engine (from the MLC-LLM team) that precompiles context-free grammars to efficient mask tables and significantly reduces per-token overhead compared to earlier approaches.

# Using lm-format-enforcer with a HuggingFace pipeline
from lmformatenforcer import JsonSchemaParser
from lmformatenforcer.integrations.transformers import build_transformers_prefix_allowed_tokens_fn
from transformers import pipeline

schema = {
    "type": "object",
    "properties": {
        "name": {"type": "string"},
        "score": {"type": "integer", "minimum": 0, "maximum": 100}
    },
    "required": ["name", "score"]
}

parser = JsonSchemaParser(schema)
hf_pipeline = pipeline("text-generation", model="mistralai/Mistral-7B-Instruct-v0.2")
prefix_fn = build_transformers_prefix_allowed_tokens_fn(hf_pipeline.tokenizer, parser)

result = hf_pipeline(
    "Rate the following candidate. Output JSON: Alice has 8 years of experience.",
    prefix_allowed_tokens_fn=prefix_fn,
    max_new_tokens=80,
)
print(result[0]["generated_text"])
Several approaches address the structured-output problem. The choice depends on the serving infrastructure, latency requirements, and how much schema guarantee is required:
SQL injection and prompt injection via constrained fields

Constrained decoding can be used to prevent injection by restricting string fields to safe character sets. However, it is not a complete injection defense: the grammar constrains the model's output, but it does not prevent an attacker from crafting an input prompt that causes the model to populate a string field with a harmful-but-schema-valid value (e.g., a valid SQL string that is nonetheless injected when interpolated into a query).
. Use parameterized queries, ORM binding, and input sanitization even when the generating model is constrained.

An adversarially crafted or unusually long partial output can push the automaton into a very deep nesting state (e.g., 1000 levels of nested arrays). Some implementations do not bound stack depth; an attacker who controls the prompt could cause out-of-memory or extreme slowdown. Mitigations:
Set max_tokens conservatively to bound the maximum output length
Add schema-level depth constraints (e.g., maxItems, maxLength) to bound automaton exploration
Use battle-tested libraries (Outlines, xgrammar) that include depth bounds in their grammar compilers

If the grammar requires a character sequence that cannot be produced by any single token or valid token combination, the automaton may reach a state with no valid tokens. Well-implemented libraries handle this with an error; poorly implemented ones may infinite-loop or produce corrupted output. Always test schemas against the model's tokenizer before deploying:

# Test that the grammar has no dead states for the target tokenizer
import outlines

try:
    gen = outlines.generate.json(model, MySchema)
    # Dry-run a short generation to exercise the automaton
    result = gen("test", max_tokens=10)
except Exception as e:
    print(f"Schema incompatible with tokenizer: {e}")
    # Simplify schema or switch to a model with a larger vocabulary
Log automaton state at failure — if generation produces an empty output or cuts off mid-field, log the final automaton state to diagnose whether max_tokens was too low or the grammar had a dead state
Monitor per-schema token overhead to catch schema changes that inflate the automaton
Use a circuit breaker: if the constrained generator produces output below a minimum length (e.g., fewer tokens than the smallest valid schema instance), treat it as a failure

Constrained decoding enforces schema validity by masking invalid-token logits to −∞ before sampling — the model cannot produce non-conforming output, no matter what its internal representations prefer.
It eliminates parse failures and retry loops entirely, making it the right choice for high-volume batch extraction and low-latency pipelines where retries are costly.

The technique requires access to the model's logit computation — self-hosted inference engines (vLLM, llama.cpp, TGI) support it natively; hosted APIs offer weaker but practical analogues via JSON mode and tool-call schemas.
Per-token overhead is real (5–25 %) and depends on grammar complexity; precompile and cache grammar processors, and monitor time-per-token to detect regressions after schema changes.
Structural correctness is guaranteed; semantic correctness is not — always apply business-logic validation (value bounds, cross-field consistency, domain rules) after constrained generation.
Keep schemas as flat and simple as the task permits; over-constraining free-text fields degrades fluency without adding useful guarantees.
Use Optional[T] = None for fields that may be absent in the source, to prevent the model from hallucinating values just to satisfy a required-field constraint.
Treat constrained output as untrusted input for downstream security purposes: parameterized queries, ORM binding, and sanitization remain necessary even when output structure is guaranteed.
